description.s: A structural editor for Hob where the user manipulates AST nodes rather than characters. The editor understands Hob's structure at all times — unbalanced parentheses, malformed expressions, and syntax errors are impossible by construction.
The editor is implemented as a
— no parsing step on load, no serialization on save.
Every edit produces a well-formed AST. There is no parse-error state.
An experienced user can build expressions nearly as fast as typing text. The structural constraints help rather than hinder.
When the user creates a hole, the editor shows what's valid in that position — available symbols, item references, special forms. The system presents choices.
The AST is rendered to look like properly formatted s-expressions with syntax colouring.
Load and save operate on compact JSON AST (
Selection replaces the text cursor. Exactly one AST node is selected at all times. The selected node is highlighted with a visible border or background colour.
Any node in the AST: a leaf (
), a container and all its contents (
: faint highlight on ancestor containers, so you can see where in the nesting you are.
: when a container is selected, both its opening and closing delimiters are emphasised.
All navigation is by keyboard. The model is tree traversal.
Move to the preceding sibling in the same container. At first child, no-op.
Move to the following sibling in the same container. At last child, no-op.
Select the containing node. At root, no-op.
If selected node is a container, select its first child. On leaves, no-op.
Jump to first child of the current parent.
Jump to last child of the current parent.
Jump to the nearest ancestor that's a direct child of root.
Jump to the next hole in document order. If no holes, no-op.
but conceptually "select more"). Can be repeated to grow.
If the current selection was expanded, contract back to the previously selected child.
If in a hole's input mode, cancel input. Otherwise select the parent.
The expansion history is a stack. Each
When the user needs to enter a new value, a
appears. A hole is a visually distinct placeholder (e.g., a highlighted empty box or a dimmed
after a selected node (inserts a sibling hole after it)
before a selected node (inserts a sibling hole before it)
) — the container is created with a hole as its first child
when a container is selected (appends a child hole at the end)
to replace the selected node (the node is replaced with a hole pre-filled with its text)
When a hole is active, the editor is in
. Keystrokes go to the hole, not to navigation.
Appends to the token buffer. Autocomplete menu filters in real time.
the current token as a node. Creates a new sibling hole after it.
create a sibling hole. Exits input mode.
Cancels input. If the hole was newly created, it is removed. If replacing, the original node is restored.
If buffer is empty: creates a list with a hole inside. If buffer has content: commits the buffer first, then creates a list sibling.
Commits the current token (if any). Exits input mode. Selects the parent container. If the delimiter doesn't match the parent container type, it is ignored (prevents structural errors).
If at start of token: switches to keyword mode (characters until Space form a keyword). If mid-token: treated as regular character.
Deletes last character from buffer. If buffer is empty, removes the hole.
When a token is committed (by Space, Enter, or a structural character), the editor determines what kind of node it is:
in a hole (or when the buffer is empty).

In string mode, all characters are literal — Space doesn't commit, parentheses don't create structure. The editor shows a text input field (or a distinct visual region) where the user types freely. Standard escapes work (
again (unescaped) ends string mode and commits the string node.
inserts a newline within the string.
The editor shows a searchable dropdown of all items in the system. The user types to filter by name or ID. The list shows the item name, type, and a preview.
Select the highlighted item, insert an item-ref node
Cancel, return to normal hole input
Select the highlighted item AND create a sibling hole (fluid continuation)

Item references are displayed in the AST as a chip showing the item's name, not the raw UUID. The chip is visually distinct (e.g., a coloured pill with the item's type icon). Clicking the chip navigates to that item.
When typing in a hole, an autocomplete dropdown appears showing contextually valid options, filtered by what's been typed so far.
bindings. Determined by walking up the AST from the hole's position.
at the top of the module.
, switch to item search. But even without
, if the typed text matches an item name, it could appear as a suggestion with an
In certain positions, the editor can show richer guidance:
: likely a function call. Show functions and special forms prominently.
: likely a binding name. Show hint "name".
: alternating key/value. Show keywords prominently for keys.
: expecting a name. Show hint "name" and suppress function suggestions.
This context awareness can be built incrementally.
Beyond hole-based input, the editor supports structural operations on existing nodes.
Remove the selected node. Siblings close the gap. If the node is a container, all children are deleted too.
Replace the selected node with a hole, pre-filled with the node's text representation.
Insert a hole after the selected node (as a sibling).
Insert a hole before the selected node (as a sibling).
. The node becomes the first child. A hole is added after it.
. A hole is added for the value.
Remove the selected container, promoting its children into the parent. Only works on container nodes.
Swap the selected node with its previous sibling.
Swap the selected node with its next sibling.
The selected container absorbs the next sibling. `(a \
The selected container ejects its last child as a sibling after it.
puts the editor in "wrap mode" where the next character determines the container type. If no valid character follows within 300ms, wrap mode is cancelled.
Copy the selected subtree to the clipboard (internal AST format + plain text as s-expression).
Insert the clipboard content as a sibling after the selection. If clipboard contains text (from outside), parse it as Hob and insert the resulting AST.
Insert a deep copy of the selected node as a sibling after it.
Each mutation pushes a snapshot (or a reversible delta) onto the undo stack. The undo stack is in-memory, lost on save/reload. Limit ~100 operations.
The AST is rendered to look like properly formatted Hob source code, with structural awareness expressed through colour and subtle visual cues.
Nodes are laid out using standard Lisp pretty-printing rules:
break after the first element, with children indented:
: forms wider than ~60 characters break to multi-line.
: name and params on first line, body indented 2 spaces.
: binding vector on first line, body indented 2 spaces.
: condition on first line, then/else each on their own line, indented 2 spaces.
: each pair on its own line, indented 2 spaces.
Hiccup vectors: tag and attrs on first line, children indented.
Muted colour, matching pair highlighted on container select
Highlighted empty region with dashed border

Item references are shown as chips with the item's name (or truncated ID if unnamed) and a small type icon. Hovering shows a tooltip with the item's type and ID. Clicking navigates to that item. In the serialised output, it becomes
on any selected node to insert a comment after it. Comments are rich text — they support the same formatting as

A comment appears as a doc node inline in the AST, visually distinct from code. When selected, it opens the rich text editor for editing. Comments are stored as part of the AST and are saved with the code.
Insert a new comment after the selected node. Opens the rich text editor.
When a comment is selected, open it for editing.
A narrow bar at the bottom showing:
: the type of each ancestor from root to selection. E.g.,
: the selected node's type and value.
: "NAV" for navigation mode, "INPUT" when in a hole, "WRAP..." when in wrap mode, "STRING" during string input.
A minimal toolbar (or right-click context menu) with structural operations for discoverability. Each button shows its keyboard shortcut.
) showing all available keyboard shortcuts in the current context.
